All right, this is Sean. I am here with Lauren. We're going to do this as a missing chapter. Lauren's had quite a solid last month and a half or so. It's one of those things. He was kind enough to volunteer to just tell the story. I think it's broadly useful for all of us who are dealing with Facebook and it's sort of idiosyncrasies and there's a lot to be learned from Lauren's experience. So what I'm going to do is have Lauren to sort of share what has been going on for him as it relates to Facebook. And then we're going to try to pull out some lessons from that. So I'm going to be asking questions, hopefully really from your perspective, from the audience, like what I think you might be interested in hearing. So Lauren, why don't you give us the 30,000 foot view of what happened in sort of whatever level of detail you want to go to and where you are right now. Sure, well, I always tell people it is a long, long, long story, but I will give you, I'll be merciful and give you kind of the, as you said, the abbreviated high level version. Long story short is I am restricted from advertising on Facebook. My personal profile is restricted, which means I can't own any ad accounts or business managers, I can't even manage ads. So for Sean working with him or for anyone else that said, hey, can you run my Facebook ads? I can't even do that. And by the way, I didn't do anything wrong and that's part of I think what we can unpack. I feel very confident that it's all just false flags. It's all just part of Facebook being a little crazy in just the way things work out. So to kind of back up, most people listening to this probably understand, but when you advertise with Facebook, you have to abide by their policies. And if you don't abide by their policies, then you can lose advertising privileges. Ads, on an ad level, they can be rejected, which is no big deal, happens all the time. If you get enough ads rejected or if you have a serious enough violation of Facebook's advertising policies, they can restrict your ad account. If you do even more, if you have more flagrant violations, then they can restrict your business manager. And the highest level of restriction is when they restrict actually your personal profile. So my personal profile is restricted as I was saying. So the funny thing is, for anyone that knows me, I'm probably almost irrationally scared of having my personal profile restricted. Now, probably rationally scared, but at the time I was one where many people were not worried about compliance, I was always pretty uptight about it. And my fear was actually like, I mean, I almost would stay up awake at night, afraid of getting my personal profile restricted because here I am and this is what I do professionally. It's a big part of my life and I didn't want that cut off because I heard stories of that happening to people and it was scary. And I thought, well, I definitely don't want that to be me. But in the back of my head, I always heard stories about people getting their personal profiles restricted because they messed up, because they violated policies, because they did shady things. And I thought, well, it probably won't happen to me because I'm doing everything by the book. But I did, I was a student of policy because of that. I worked really hard to actually understand Facebook compliance and I feel I have a pretty deep knowledge. Crazy enough, this saga's given me an even deeper knowledge of some compliance issues, but I have taken so much training and have talked to so many people and have learned a lot about compliance. So by the way, if anyone has any questions, ironically, considering my situation, you might not think it's true. But I actually understand a lot about compliance and I'm happy to help if people have questions. But despite being scared, I ended up getting personally restricted. So here's kind of the short version. Prior to the, this was towards the end of October. So about a week before the elections here in the United States, Facebook was ramping up their security. A lot of their ad compliance, a lot of their ad review process is done with AI. So and they're increasingly going more and more towards it just being automated. It's just happening without human involvement. And those AI restrictions and those AI rejections, often there are false flags. As we moved closer to the election, they kind of were ramping up security. So just seeing what I saw in Facebook groups and hearing from other advertisers, there were examples of people, just a plethora of ad rejections and ad restrictions. Account restrictions and accounts getting shut down, all sorts of nasty stuff as Facebook ramped up their security prior to the election. Well, my philosophy, as I've mentioned in the traffic engine is I always get ads approved, always. I clear my name, so to say. So if you were to check my accounts a week before the election, you would see I had zero rejected ads. Well, Thursday before the election, so just a few days before the election, I woke up and one of the accounts that I work with, we had over 700 ads get rejected overnight. Now all of these ads had been previously approved and had been running. Many of them we ran months ago and it turned off months ago. But what happened was the landing page got flagged and when that happened, every single ad connected to that landing page got flagged. This client was using click funnels, which I think was related to part of the issue. But some of the way click funnels handles their links. But anyway, we had 700 ads get rejected and the reason for the rejection was circumventing policies. For somehow the reason that click funnels or the way that click funnels handles links, it appeared to be circumventing policies. So we started to appeal some of the ads. They came back approved. We started to appeal some more of the ads. They came back approved, appealed some more of the ads. They came back approved. So over the course of the weekend, I'd whittled down 700 some rejected ads to maybe 300 or 400. Well then on Monday, the day before the election here in the US, I woke up to see that my personal profile was restricted. The strange thing was that no ad account was shut down. No business manager was shut down. It was just my personal profile. I had never really heard of that happening before. I'd normally heard of personal profile restrictions happening when you did something really bad, when you had had multiple accounts being shut down. Maybe even multiple business managers. I'd never heard of a personal profile being shut down and the ad accounts and the business manager being fine. So I suspect that, oh, this is just a bug. No big deal. We'll appeal it and we'll go back to normal. But I said just to be sure, I spent that day, that Monday, kind of just getting my fairs in order, making sure, what am I, could it be this? Could it be this? I definitely appealed all of those ads. There were about, as I said, about 300, 400 ads still left that were rejected in that one account and I appealed all of those. And then I changed my profile picture, my former profile picture. I had had on there for like 10 years. It was a stock photo of like a Bruce Lee statue. And I started to think, I mean, could it just be a false flag that my profile picture made me look like a fake profile? I mean, I was just befuddled. Like what could it possibly be? So updated some things in my profile. Anything I could do to make it look like, okay, I'm a real person and maybe this is the reason for the false flag. So had all those ads appealed, many of them started to come back approved. My history with ad appeals is they always come back within a day or two with this account that I've been working with where we had the 700 ads. Whenever we had gone through our previous rounds of appealing ads, they had come back within a day or within a 36 hours. So I started to see some of the ads come back approved. And by the way, every single ad had come back approved at this point. Every single ad, you know, 300, 400, 500 out of 700, every single ad that we had appealed came back as a false flag and it was indeed a compliant and it was okay and it was approved and everything was fine. So I appealed on Tuesday, the day of the election. I thought about maybe waiting, but I thought, eh, you know, it's the day of the election. Facebook is probably not as worried now that it's the day of the election and instead of right before it. So I'll just go ahead and appeal it, appeal that midday, that day, and we just thought, okay, this will surely it's a bug and I'll come back. Okay. Well, then what happened was the ad appeal process slowed down. I had all those ads appealed and they were in review. And when I appealed my personal profile, I said, I'm not really sure why this is restricted, why my personal profile is restricted. It might be because of these ad rejections that I got. But by the way, all of those have come back approved and I have an open support ticket for the rest of the ads. So, you know, hopefully everything's okay there. But these ad approvals, these ad appeals, it was a painfully slow process. I've never had it take that long. I had appealed those ads on Monday. Well, then it was Tuesday and then it was Wednesday and then it was Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday. It was over a week and a half that these ads were still under review. And the whole time I was a little worried. I'm like, I didn't think that it would really affect my personal profile at the time because I just thought my personal profile had to be a bug. It made no sense that it was those rejections because if it was those rejections, why didn't that ad account go down? Why didn't that business manager go down? And furthermore, I was connected that business manager as a partner. That's one kind of weird fact to mention. I was connected as a partner. And the whole reason you connect as a partner is so that you're theoretically protected so that stuff like this doesn't happen to you. So anyway, considered that the ad rejections probably weren't the reason for my personal profile being restricted, but I wanted to see those ads get approved anyway. So we kept reaching back out to support saying, hey, can you take a look at these ads? Can you take a look at these ads? And for a week and a half, they basically said, no, just wait, you've got an open support ticket for these ads. Just wait. So we waited and we waited and we waited reaching out to them about every 48 hours for a week and a half. Then it was Thursday. This was a week and a half after the election, a week and a half after I'd been restricted, about a week and a half after about two weeks after all these ads were under review. About two weeks after all the ads have been rejected, but a week and a half after I had appealed on that Thursday, I got the results of my appeal back for the personal restriction. And I saw that email come through and I got really excited. And so I thought, great, I'm going to get my access back. And I looked at the email and it said that something that an automated message to the effect of your appeal was rejected. And then we've determined that you did violate policies. So your account is restricted permanently. This is our final answer. So I was like, oh, that's, that's really unexpected. But I reached out to some friends who had been, I'd been talking to you through this process. I have a friend who previously had his account shut down. And he, he had eventually he got it back. And for him, he did not get it back on the first appeal. He got it back on like the second or third appeal. So I heard from multiple people like, oh, that's normal, you know, it's normal for you to get your first appeal rejected, just keep appealing. So I thought, okay, no big deal. These ads, by the way, were still showing rejected when I when my personal profile was was reviewed. And I got the email back that I was restricted that my appeal was rejected. These ads were still showing rejected because that that support ticket was was still open. Some like great. So I finally got those ads approved, finally got those ads approved. I reached back out to support to concierge chat support to basically say, hey, you know, I have this weird situation. I've had this support ticket open for these ads. My appeal for my personal profile was considered while that support ticket was open. But now all of those ads have come back approved 100% of those ads came back approved and I'm back now to the point where I have zero ads rejected in my account. So can I get my personal profile reviewed again, because I had the support ticket open the whole time and it took Facebook a week and a half. And, you know, I have this very, I think compelling story about why a second review is is merited. But the chat support told me that they couldn't do it. They said, it basically it's not that they don't want to. It's not that they don't think I deserve one is that they do not have the ability to do anything. Their hands are tied. And I asked, well, can you talk to a manager? Can you somehow escalate it? Is there, can you send an email? Is there anything you can do? And what the chat support, what concierge support told me was that no, there was nothing they could do that Facebook was just very departmentalized and they cannot get in touch with that team. There is nothing they can do. The only way to appeal your restricted profile is to click that button that you get when you get an appeal when you get your profile restricted, you click that button to get an appeal, and you get to do that one time and that's it. And after that there is no way for concierge support to get in touch with that team. So the talk actually scheduled the next day with a Facebook marketing expert. I had this scheduled for weeks long before my personal profile got restricted. So I talked with that Facebook marketing expert. I talked to him for probably 45 minutes and he's actually a nice guy and to his credit, the marketing experts by the way are like the lowest level reps that you can get. And their advice is kind of a joke. They're the boat of jokes in the Facebook marketing world. But I thought maybe he can help me out with this. So I explained to the guy what happened, I explained to him my story and he felt for me and I explained to him. Also, there's one significant point I've left out, which is that I'm a Christian, and I am an ethical person and I'm a person with a very strict conscience. So I'm not going to circumvent policies. If Facebook says you are restricted and you cannot advertise, I'm not going to circumvent that. There are ways that people who don't have ethical qualms can circumvent that. You can create a fake profile. You can use your brother's profile or your mom's profile and have them start an account and you can use a VPN and get a new laptop and do screen shares. And there's all sorts of shady ways that people get around that. But I don't find that to be ethical. And so for me, when Facebook restricts me that restriction stands and that that I'm not going to do it. I'm not going to I'm done right I'm done with Facebook advertising if I can't get get access back. So I told this Facebook marketing expert that I'm like, look, you know, this is this is my career. This is my livelihood like economically like I'm, you know, I'm a little worried right now for what this means financially. And I'm not going to circumvent policies. I don't think that's ethical. So if I don't get this back, I mean that's all she wrote. And I explained to him the story that I've the long and when this story I just told you about how this happened. He felt for me and he understood that yeah, this is crazy like this should not happen. But so he said that there wasn't a lot that he could personally do that it's true his hands are tied. Partially due to coven partially just due to the way that Facebook works is they they're not meeting in an office somewhere. So it's not like you can walk down the hall and talk to someone. There's only so much they can do they they have such limited access to the rest of their company that he can file a ticket with concierge support which is basically the chat support. But that's that's all he can do and if it's if that's if that doesn't work he doesn't know what else he can do. So he did tell me that he would file a ticket with concierge support on my behalf, and that because it came from him, it was an internal ticket, and it was treated I guess a little more seriously. And he could also add some context to the ticket to basically if if concierge support can do anything he was more or less advocating that they do something. So he did that and I told him I'm like what happens if this doesn't work and he's like well you know reach back out. Keep me in the loop I'll do what I can. So he opened up a ticket with concierge support I heard back about two days later that they couldn't do anything that, as I've said before you know their departmentalized. They do not have the ability to get in touch with that team. And they always have the you know the kind of silly statements like we apologize for the inconvenience I'm going to close this case now have a good day but it's just kind of, I don't know a little aggravating. But that was it so I reached back out to that Facebook marketing rep via email I haven't heard heard back from him. And I feel for the guy I'm not, I'm not saying anything that you know me and against him or disparaging him I honestly think it's just Facebook is so large and massive and very very broken up in different departments where the right hands does not have the ability to communicate to the left hand. So that that was that. Since then I think I've emailed. Maybe maybe I reached out to chat support again honestly I can't remember it's kind of all all blurced together I did have a friend who has a mid tier rep that he was able to put me in touch with an agency rep, one of the agencies I've worked with. And we I reached out to that rep, they kind of gave me some places to go some things to try none of that was successful some forms to try to fill out none of that was successful. And that mid tier agency rep basically told me yet. Yeah, there's nothing I can do my hands are tied. I do not have the ability to get in touch with that team. I wish there was something I can do. I wish I had better news. But that's where we stand. Well, throughout this experience. I also had some connections to higher level reps. One rep that I worked with actually several months ago was a higher level rep. I reached out to her when this restriction had happened and said, I'm freaking out a little can you help me, you know, and so she said she would look into things. Well, I heard back from her. This, this had been, you know, two or three weeks later at this point I heard back from her she's just doing this because she's a nice person and because I developed a little bit of a, you know, professional friendship with her as we we work together on account but I'm no longer under her wings so she doesn't need to do anything she's not obligated she's just being nice. She reached out to a couple weeks later. And it was just kind of updating me and asking me if I had heard anything from my end and she said that she had reached out to the team to see why I was restricted, and they had emailed her back. I'm assuming these are the people in charge of the personal profile restrictions. They had emailed her back saying, yes, because of these ad IDs, and they had given her some ad IDs. And then she checked herself and said, well the ad IDs are now showing approved. So that's really confusing to me. So I'm going to reach back out to the team and see if there's something we can do about getting you a second review. And that's where we currently stand but the good news there from that email was that the restriction Facebook pretty much confirmed the restriction was because of those rejected ad IDs and those ad IDs now are approved. And I think some hidden suspicious thing that's going to sneak up on me. It's pretty much everywhere confirmed that it was false flags that hopefully I can someday, hopefully someday I can get a second review. So where we that that brings us up to the current day. I have a few contacts that I think have some higher level reps that that maybe I can. I'm not calling in every favor of I ever have yet, although I'll get to that I'm kind of at this point I'm taking it a little more slowly. My concern at this point is getting it back and not necessarily getting it back quickly. I think we're past the window of getting it back quickly. I've been in the office for almost a month and a half since when I had it restricted. But I'm cautiously hopeful that I will be able to get a second review somehow, even if it takes six months even if it takes a year. And I'm hopeful that if I, if I do get that second review that I will get my access back. But it's just until then it's a it's a waiting game. I'm not going to get an email, you know, right when we're done with this call, I may get the email that I got my access back, and I may not get it for another year. And that's pretty much brings us to the here and now. And there's there's so much to learn here and so much to be frustrated by. I mean, I think I've mentioned this repeatedly in different formats in different contexts but I think that a high level mastermind at one point and this is people in the room are paying very big bucks to be there and somebody asked the question, you know, like how many of you have lost sleep in the last week, concerned about your Facebook ad account being shut down. There are like 25 people around the main table and I think it was like 23 people I just counted like that's how many people were like really staying up late and this is this problem is pervasive. You know, clients Lauren and I have worked with others I've worked with over the years stories that you hear this is this is not uncommon. It's a real barrier to working with Facebook. You know, jokingly, I, this is certainly isn't a lot of room for humor in this but I don't, I don't think the fact that Lawrence having trouble with Facebook and the, and Zuckerberg being under the FT, FTC guidelines for breaking up Facebook and I think those are coincidental I think Lauren is flexing his muscle and he's not not willing to not willing to say that just yet but yeah but usually you know I think it's important to do sort of a point counterpoint so the way you've handled it is not at all how most people handle it. I think that's something about his ethical framework and he's certainly among the most ethical people I've ever met in my life, you won't find anybody with higher integrity, but that's not what happens for most people. The system, the incentives of the Facebook advertising system really drive people to do things that seem at face value to be crazy until you hear a story like Lauren so what I've seen happen over and over again is somebody gets an ad account. They get restricted. So they create a new ad account, which is a violation of Facebook's policies but they, they do it because it's, there's too much friction in the system to get the ad account fixed so you just get a new ad account, and then they do that several times. All of a sudden, some other level of access maybe business manager gets compromised or maybe you lose business manager, like I have several times programmatically for AI and 48 hours later Facebook comes back and says, whoops sorry, our mistake. Knock on wood. So far that's been okay. But then, where this often leads is in I've seen this happen so many times where it's the situation that Lauren described your using mom's Facebook account through a VPN with disposable credit card it gets crazy. And you wonder like how did I get here, like what happened. And I want to be clear that's, I haven't done that. I've worked with lots of clients you have I don't have any judgment for anybody because incentives drive behavior and Facebook's incentives, clearly are driving the behavior that strangely isn't the behavior that they want they're creating the problems that they themselves say that they don't want. So very strange situation. And I do want to just do the, the condensed version because I think it's there's a legal principle in the United States and it's not a legal principle probably not the right description but it's this idea. That's called the fruit from the poison tree right you can't kick somebody's door down without a warrant and then find drugs and then say well, we found drugs so therefore you're guilty you know because you didn't have a warrants everything that happened after that doesn't matter it's fruit from the poison tree you can't use those things well that's exactly what happened here is Facebook did something programmatically with AI that caused a bunch of ads to get disapproved incorrectly. They shouldn't have been disapproved and over time they were reapproved. But in the midst of that you got caught up in it and you lost access from that thing that Facebook later realized was incorrect on their part. I mean it's crazy. It's absolutely crazy. Not only that but the only time this is the only time I've ever had a support ticket for ad review take a week and a half. I've never had to go over two days ever. And the one time that you know literally was the most important ad review of my life. It was a week and a half you know so all in all like I don't. Everyone that I've worked with at Facebook everyone that I've talked to has been you know I hold in high regards I think people are honorable they do what they can and I don't even fault Facebook for having high security I they have such a magnifying glass on them particularly right before the elections I get it and I feel for them because no matter what you did you were going to get I mean everyone in the world is looking and they're going to talk about elections being stolen by Russia or you know whatever. I totally understand why Facebook did what they did. And that doesn't actually upset me the heightened security. It is inconvenient you know you wish that maybe they leaned more on human reviewers and less on AI. But but I don't mind them ramping up their security I don't mind them restricting personal stuff I don't mind them. I don't mind false flags, because I think just the stakes were high, we're that high close to the election. What bothers me is just the fact that I can't get a second appeal. Like, it takes, you know, an act of Congress to get me a second review, and furthermore, what what is a little upsetting. Although I'm very blessed, and I am cautiously hopeful I'll get my account reinstated. That's only because I've got connections to high level reps that not everyone has. The mom and pop business is going to get access to concierge support and that's it. If I would have been a mom and pop business. I would, I wouldn't have, I wouldn't have any any recourse and that that's kind of, I think, unfortunate. And that's, and I think the another issue here to draw some attention to is that, you know, this Andre and I talk all the time about systems theory, you know, the effects emerge from the system that are not present in any part of the system. That's really the crux of systems theory that you things happen that nowhere in the system does the, can you point to and say oh that's what did it. It's an emergent effect. So if you, if you think about what Facebook wants. So from an advertising perspective what is Facebook optimizing for and they're optimizing for happy advertisers, who gets some sort of measurable return on investment. So they'll continue to advertise and they also are optimizing for happy users of Facebook so that the ads are not too intrusive they're not full of, you know, profanity or nudity they're not bothering people they're not to swarmy or anything like that. They're both sides there. So Facebook should be optimizing for advertisers who play by its rules. I mean that's makes perfect sense. Yet here we have a situation where one of Facebook's most most ethical advertisers who is actually knows policy and is adjusting behavior to be in line with that policy is penalized by the system. I'm certain there's no, there's no one at Facebook, trying to do this yet their system has produced this effect and that's it's incredibly unfortunate. I wish this hadn't been true for as long as it has been true. This has been true. You know, I've, I've been working with Facebook advertising really. I don't know what the first day we used it but it was early on when it looked nothing like it is now, but it was just a novelty. And this has been true every single day since like this. Everybody has the same angst around Facebook around access to Facebook, everybody. And it's, it's incredibly unfortunate. So, I guess the question I would ask you next Lauren is, you know, what, what insights did you pull from this. You've pulled a million of them, one of which is, you know, maybe that one time that the 48 hours wasn't going to be quite enough. But you've also had some, you have, you have a couple of things you have some distance and time from the event. So it gives you some perspective but you also have a level headed presence of mind that most people wouldn't have in this situation. So from that perspective of some time and keeping yourself calm throughout all of this and looking for solutions like what, throw a couple insights our way like what anything you do differently anything that you are just kind of just run with it. And so one of the things I thought about that is I was preparing for this talk like what what lessons rather than just tell a cool story and then like, all right, that was entertaining. What lessons can people take away from this and I think here are lessons that everyone, I think can learn from this and here's what I've learned personally. So at the tactical level, definitely, I didn't know you only got one appeal. So that's true for personal profiles from what I've heard it hasn't always been that way because I've heard of people getting multiple appeals in the past. It may not be that way in six months, it may be totally different, but at least right now, you get one appeal on the personal profile, you click that button, which, you know, in the account quality it shows you you're restricted. It says, you know, click here to appeal, you click that button and that's it. That's that's all she wrote you can put in a contextual message after that they don't pay attention. There's ways you can like reply to the support ticket like they're not looking at that you click a button and that's it and you get to do it one time. I think I've heard that's actually true also for business managers, I've never personally had my business manager go down I know Sean BDM. You know, your business manager has gone down like two or three times, false flags. Yeah, three or four, I think. Yeah. So I've heard though that that works similarly where you get, you get one appeal. So my, my advice is, don't, you know, don't waste that appeal, take stock and get everything square beforehand. All the ads do whatever you can audit every funnel, every every ad every landing page look for ad rejections think of anything that could possibly be and rectify those before you appeal because that may very well be your one shot. Obviously that's kind of the biggest tactical learning. Secondly is, you know, in terms of getting protecting yourself and partner access and everything else like I, you know, I, I'm not sure that that is the protection that I thought it was. I don't really know how to go about doing that in a way in the future. I think maybe if you have an agency. I was a partner, but I was an admin, I'm a, it's like a one person deal. So I'm the admin of the business manager. I place the ads. I'm had another, I had a backup admin on the business manager, which is my, my wife, but she was never active in the business manager. So that might have a reason maybe Facebook just interpreted that is not, not a true partner access. So maybe if someone wanted to protect themselves, actually granting like employee access. Maybe that wouldn't have been the same. I don't even know. One thing I did learn to, and I forgot to mention this is it was important, I believe that, well, actually two things. It was important, I believe that my ads were rejected for circumventing policies, because I think Facebook treats that much more seriously than if it had been rejected for, you know, some other minor reason. MLM or, you know, income claims or something like that. There's no good reason don't get me wrong. You don't want to be rejected. But I think circumventing policies, particularly right around the election. I think it's something that they took more seriously. And I think they're more inclined to go after you personally on your personal profile when you've circumvented policies. That was one lesson. Another lesson is account quality and this if someone's not familiar with compliance, this may just go over their head and but if you look in the account quality screen. I learned this that Facebook, when all my ads got rejected that one Thursday, Facebook does not treat those ads as if they were rejected that one Thursday. Because it's a, they will retroactively reject that ad, right. So in other words, they will treat that ad as if it should have been rejected when I launched it months ago, and they will treat that rejection as occurring months ago. The reason is that when you look at my account history, not only did I have hundreds of cases of circumventing policies, but it was not like they all happened on the same day. It was like a few this week and a few the next week and a few the next week that were spread out in time, because Facebook will treat that rejection as to the date that the ad was launched. So I think that was one reason why it went straight to my personal profile was because it was spread out over a time period of rejections, and it was circumventing policies so for whatever, whatever it's worth. That may be something tactically to keep in mind to, to be very careful about that. Other than that, strategically, I think that there would be a real danger I think for anyone putting their eggs in one basket. I think it's one of those things you anyone I think could have recognized beforehand and said that but you kind of have that bubble of invulnerability like I will never happen to me. You know, I'll never get my business shut down and I'll never get my ad account shut down or whatever. I, I always kind of viewed people that talked about accounts getting shut down and personal particularly personal profiles they always say, Oh, I never did anything wrong I never did anything wrong. But then when you you dig a little further they're like, Well, okay, there was that one ad I launched for something super spammy and that other ad that I launched for another, you know, non compliant product I mean, when you dig deeper there's there's always a reason for that and so I always view the personal profile restriction as kind of a scarlet letter. There's a reason for it but I think now, you know, this can happen, even if you, you know, you have been abiding by their policies so strategically, don't put all your eggs in one basket, you know, Sean obviously has great teaching on Google, YouTube, organic, other channels, whatever it is, I would be reluctant to put all my, all my bags in the Facebook basket. If I were a business and as a professional. Also, you know, I'm very blessed that I've been able to kind of pivot in a way, but, but if you are a professional ad manager and your, your career your livelihood depends on pushing buttons in the platform. That is a, that's a dangerous place to be in terms of what I've learned personally, and this is maybe not so much the traffic engine lesson but kind of the life lesson is I had considered for a while, you know, maybe I can do a little more of this stuff, do a little more of this ad management stuff I can, I can be an agency basically an agency of one, because I, I feel that I have the, you know, the copy ability and the creative ability and the strategic ability and the ad management ability like I can actually produce a value for a client. And for some number of months, it was kind of like, alright, maybe that's kind of where my career is going is is the agency route of being an agency of one. But I just, I had a hesitance simply because of I, in large part Facebook compliance. And another large part was, you know, Facebook is a huge platform now but what about five years from now, what about 10 years from now, I mean imagine if you had based your career on my space, you know, so there were a few reasons for reluctance about going all in to Facebook advertising, and I had been thinking about that and mulling that over and praying about it and honestly, this is, this is my answer that the day before I found out that my personal profile was permanent, that my restriction, you know, or quote unquote permanent. The day before I was actually at church and I was talking with a guy who had cancer in his 20s. And he said that, believe it or not, it's the best thing that ever happened to them, it happened to him because he was able to confront some issues in his life and and you know, just deal with some issues he needed to deal with and and grab hold of what he needed to grab hold of and really examine himself. And that was kind of, for me, what's happened with this is it's been a real cause for introspection and a real cause for examining, you know, my career and examining what I do. And I feel that although it's a step back, it's going to hopefully one day be two steps forward is the way I feel about it. So honestly, the more I have had the time to digest it and to look back less and less am I looking at it as a bad thing and more and more is maybe an unpleasant fork in the road but ultimately a good thing. And something we didn't mention that and I and I don't want to disclose who the advertiser was, but but I do want to mention that this was not in the advertiser was not in a market that really is under any kind of scrutiny. It's not one of the, you know, sort of typical markets that really runs into lots of trouble on Facebook. This is an advertiser has been doing it right since day one. Ethical great product consumer ish type of a product broad audience. So there's like the things that could have been warning signs they just weren't there that weren't present in this which makes it. It makes it all the more problematic. So and it's interesting because I've had this conversation now a similar conversation with several people who are Facebook specialists and I think there is a. I don't want to mix metaphors here. I was going to say a crisis of faith. But there's a, I do think there is that Facebook has created a bit of a crisis was with many of its advertisers, because they're not, they're not dependable, you know, they, it's one of those things where you can do everything right and still suffer and you can also do things wrong and not suffer. And you can also do things wrong and not suffer. That's one of the examples I've used repeatedly is the Motley Fool. It's just my favorite example. The Motley Fool for a long time. I don't know if they still do. They had a ad group or they had an ad running that had for a pot stock, something or other blueprint. They had an ad running forever that had an image of a marijuana plant and you can go to the Facebook's ad policies crystal clear. You can't use images of pot plants in your ads. Yet the Motley Fool did it for as far as I know for 18 months, two years at least they were using that ad and I know somebody who was also individually banned from Facebook because he used a an image of a pot plant in an ad for a pot stock newsletter financial newsletter. It's the exact same thing and his appeal essentially was well, you know, yes, I know it's, you know, the policies say we can't do that but the Motley Fool is an enormous advertiser and they've been doing it. I've seen him do it at the end of this ad has been running for 18 months and Facebook was like those, those two things are not, are not related. And it's like, well, but of course they're related right if you as an advertiser look and see like okay somebody's doing this thing and they've done it for 18 months. And now I want to do the same thing and you kick me off the platforms. It's a very strange, very strange situation. Yeah, to give a little more perspective on that this account that I got all these ad rejections in I'd been managing it. I'd probably say six months, seven months. I had not had a single rejected ad. And I don't mean like I'm not even talking about ever having a rejection like not like I get rejected and I peel it and it comes back okay like never a rejection ever. And this happened. So that that that just shows you that yeah you're never, you're never clear of that and then in terms of the Motley Fool yeah actually was looking I think today or yesterday. If you go to ad library you can see that ads still in their history, because it was related to social issues. Facebook has likes transparency about those. So it's permanently in their ad library you can go look and see and they have, I would say a dozen different pot stock related ads that show marijuana leaves or, you know, like, you know, buds and all sorts of totally non compliant images. It's not subtle it's not like there's kind of a little, you know, watermark and you're like, huh, is that a pot plant it's like, it's a picture of a pot plan it. It's ridiculous. So I think what we'll do here I didn't tell Lauren I was going to do this but I'm going to, I'm going to do this. I've thought about this is I've had a lot of colleagues and friends who have mentioned a similar conversation. So there, before I get into the like, what advice would I give Lauren, and it's advice he didn't ask for so it's the worst kind of advice but sort of, I would use Lauren broadly speaking. But before I do that one thing I want to make clear to all of you are listening to this is don't start freaking out that you're going to lose your ad account tomorrow and you're going to be suspended from Facebook. If you do things that violate policies then yes the clock is ticking. I say that's everybody there's no you're not going to trick Facebook for very long. They will find out if you are violating policies they will find out and they will respond with prejudice period. That's just it's going to happen eventually so if, but if you're not doing things wrong if you're not violating policies you're taking the time to do it. If you're taking the time to do it right. Then, let's recognize the situation for what it is is a very strange combination of events, one we had the heightened awareness of Facebook and all of its issues around the US election. So, a programmatic error, followed by another really strange circumstance with which instead of taking 48 hours to get ads approved it was a week and a half, and then there was an individual disapproval that happened to fall within that period of time so lots of stars and it doesn't make it any easier for Lauren have gone through it and continuing to live through it. But from your perspective listening to this, don't freak out unless you're doing something wrong, which case you should be freaking out. There, and there's also, you know, this is goes this another lesson in here is keep your accounts squeaky clean in the traffic engine I can remember exactly which module was one of the Facebook modules where Lauren goes through step by step, what to do to get an ad approved and do an inspirational quote at some point do not let your account accumulate disapproved ads really pay a lot of attention to that keep your accounts squeaky clean. So, lessons there don't freak out but now I'm going to switch roles for a little bit and I know Lauren will play along here but this is this is how I've thought about it. I would advise somebody in Lauren's position. And because I know Lauren really well and we're good friends like I thought about this very deeply that Facebook is wanted to be a really good Facebook market or Facebook advertiser actually has embedded in it. A lot of different skill sets if you if you're really broadly skilled as Lauren is, it has a lot of skill sets, it has writing compelling marketing copy. It has identifying audiences and really thinking about like what kind of interests and psychographic and demographic features are characteristics would make an audience and what kind of size of an audience would you want. Would you optimize if you were thinking about what you wanted somebody ultimately to do, you know where the inflection points in that so there's there's a lot of strategic thinking. There's thinking about the presentation of an offer when you're presenting parts of it in hinting at it in an ad and that's congruent with the landing pages and other content in between before the offer is made. And so I think that there's this enormous body of skills that all funnel into being able to advertise, be a high quality advertiser, a high quality advertising professional on Facebook. And because I know Lauren I know that he could take those skills and leave that particular platform and apply either those skills individually, or apply those skills and to ramp up in combine to wrap up to a new platform, ramp up a new platform. So, I think that the easy answer to say in a situation like this or the easy recommendation to say is like, I'll just go do YouTube right so YouTube seems to be the Wild West go do that well YouTube is it's not the same. That's a great answer. And that could, Lauren do it sure. Right the first five seconds of a good YouTube ad kind of match the conceptually the you know having a good hook the first two lines in a Facebook ad sure we could do this intellectual exercise and map those two things but I think really the, the lowest of the recommendations in terms of how I would think about it. I think where I would start the highest value to a client is, is the strategic perspective. So I think where Lauren could start is he could be a strategist. And so I think that that 30,000 foot view and position himself as a strategist, whether it has anything to do with Facebook or not, just in general, marketing strategist digital marketing strategist. I think there are also skill sets that are components of it and I'll just focus on one which I think Lauren excels which is writing. So, to write a really compelling 1200 word Facebook ad requires a knowledge of writing that's, it's tights precise. It's a marketing writing, it's not just, it's not creative writing but it's, it's writing to get somebody to take an action if they're the right person. So that's a skill set Lauren has that he could broadly explore doesn't have doesn't mean he doesn't kind of go to be a copywriter and so I'm suggesting at all and there's so many ways that that writing the ability to write good marketing copy copy that takes somebody from point A to a desired future reality that they have that's there's so many ways to do that and so many ways to monetize that as a skill. So that that's that would also be my broad recommendations is, you know, look at the component parts see which things light you up. I suspect that anytime Lawrence going to get the email it says everything's back on track. That's, this is just such a ridiculous example, but at the same time, if that doesn't happen. I think whatever happens next is going to be infinitely better. I'm actually kind of excited to see what that's what that's going to be and what direction you're going to go. So any parting thoughts before we wrap up today Lauren for I don't want to say thanks before we wrap up just I really appreciate you being willing to discuss this with with our audience and, and to discuss it really at the end of the day. I think it would be easy to just kind of not want anybody to know about this and keep your fingers crossed until you're on the other side of it and then pretend nothing happened. I think it takes tremendous amount of courage cannot say hey this is what happened. It is what it is here's how I'm dealing with it I hope it gets better. So, so thank you for that anything else you want to mention before we wrap up for today. I appreciate letting you, letting me come on and bore everyone to tears with the story. It was probably way too long but scared scared scared everybody as much as it is. It is an interesting story I get you know it's weird and I have the, you know, I've been able to think a lot about it and look back and you mentioned kind of almost being excited and and that's almost where I am now in a weird way. Although it's, it's a jolt, not being able to have any personal profile access to advertising. Really, all that means is that I can't push buttons, but I can still write I can still come up with a strategy and I've actually kind of been able to consult with people which truth be told I kind of like I'm still managing the ads that someone else is the one waking up at 6am to check the accounts and checking it throughout the day and I just kind of assist that person with decision making and and copy and creative and strategy, which is, I think a higher value skill than the I've been able to go through the buttons and ad management so still been able to do that and it's almost the type thing where, if I were to, I've been forced to, to make a necessary ending, you know, I'm reading Henry cloud, necessary in endings and I was forced to kind of pivot in my career, which is probably actually a really good thing, but it took this to to force that to make your kind words shown and actually I feel pretty good about it, like, like the gentleman I knew who had cancer and said it was the best thing that happened in a really weird way not to compare this to something as serious as cancer but in a really weird way. I, I'm already looking back and saying well this is kind of will be a good thing. It's going to be a, you know, it's an it'll be an interesting couple weeks. But I'm confident and I also want to echo what you said about not to panic. I think it's a good story so that people know that this exists and not to be complacent. And hopefully people can learn some some lessons from this, but I would agree. I'm, this is kind of a fluke. I don't think this is the normal thing so for people listening. Until election 2024, where you're probably okay and just, you know, keep keep studying policy and do what you can to remain compliant and it'll all work out because I, I actually am fairly hopeful that I'll get access back eventually and at this point. It's not actually because I want to advertise anymore. It's not that I want to push buttons anymore. It's that mainly I want to get my name cleared. I just want to have that to where I can be like, I see guys I'm really not a scammer. I really didn't do anything wrong. But I wanted to thank you, Sean for letting me tell this story and hopefully people got something out of it and actually walked away with some benefit and kind of cool to talk not just about Facebook tactically but compliance tactically but also to think about, you know, life lessons and overarching business lessons. So hopefully this was this is valuable and it's been a it's been a good conversation for me so appreciate it Sean. And I'll just echo something that you said before we wrap up here which you know we covered was weird this year for all of us and there was there was certainly a period of time. I just had a very strange professional situation early this year where I had three contracts renewing at the end of February, which really represented a very substantial part of my income. And when COVID hit all three of those, they just didn't happen. And I sort of watched it unfold over. That was really two to three weeks, I guess that it wasn't. It was just very strange. But there was this moment and I don't know where who I stole the idea from but that I remember thinking, like wondering what amazing thing was about to happen. Because this idea that I borrowed from somebody I can't remember who it was I feel bad not being able to cite the person but that this idea that life and life happens for you instead of to you. So I had that first whatever reason and I don't know how I had the presence of mind to think about that that I had. I had this thought that something amazing was about to happen. And, you know, three weeks, two or three weeks later the traffic engine was launched and less than a month later, Andre and I became business partners. So I had, which for not that was not something that we had been discussing at the time so amazing things often happen on the other side of adversity. So I am knowing Lauren 100% certain that however this turns out something more amazing is on the other side of it. So thanks again to Lauren. Thanks all of you for listening. Just again this is a missing chapter in the traffic engine that I think Lauren and I can agree that we we hoped we would never have as a chapter. But it is what it is. It's happened and hopefully you've found some value of it. Thanks everybody. Hopefully we have an update some point that I got it back eventually. That's right.
